speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 18, 1995. I am in Branford, Connecticut, in the United States. I am interviewing Selma Engel, who is a survivor of Sobibor. This interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Are-- are you going to signal me by touching my back? OK. Selma, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is May the 15, 19-- May the 15th, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what your growing up was like? Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three older brothers. I was the youngest and the only girl in the family. And we had a very nice youth. Holland is very nice. It was before the war-- was a very nice country to live. And I did on sport. I had a canoe. I went to Zionist camp. I was-- every Sunday we had meetings. And it was a very nice life in Holland-- very nice. Yeah-- Holland is nice country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your three brothers' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mauritz, Marthyn, and Bram.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's business during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a hotel-- a kosher hotel-- in Zwolle. It was the only kosher hotel like in the state of Overijssel. It's like a province from Holland. And they did financially-- and-- when I grow up very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you got older, did you help in the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died. I helped a little bit. But my father died in 1941. And after that, we stayed a year in the hotel. And I helped a lot in the hotel. Ah, no, I didn't. I was just-- I didn't. When I was younger, and before the war, I don't think that I helped much in the hotel, no. But it was a very nice life, because we had the Jewish salesmen, what traveled in Holland, in [NON-ENGLISH]. They stayed overnight in our hotel. So it was always very nice. And there was always weddings in the hotel. And it was a hotel and a cattle market. Cattle market is a tremendous business in Europe. And it was one of the biggest cattle markets-- what I just read in the newspaper about-- in Holland, before the war. And so all these people-- a lot-- and it was a lot-- in hand of the Jews. And they came all to the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very interesting. I was always the only girl there. And a lot of-- everybody was always very nice. And it was-- was an interesting life. It was-- now I look back, was it an interesting life. Can you tell me about your mother? Did your mother help--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was the main person in the hotel, really. She's a very good businesswoman. And she was the one that would hold everything together. And she was really a-- a wonderful, nice person. I really didn't see her so much, because she always worked in the hotel. And I think I was brought up by the maids, because I was always-- you know, maids in the hotel would have to help cleaning. But my mother was a very capable and very intelligent woman. She was president from-- like, from Hadassah. This was not Hadassah-- it was another name-- and-- in Holland. And she-- she was a-- a very-- she was very orthodox. And she was also one-- was a [NON-ENGLISH]. That was-- in the Jewish people, they-- when somebody died, and only very orthodox people help in washing them and put-- you know-- for the dead. And that's what she did, too. So she was-- she was really a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about what your house was like, what books were around, if you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house was very big, because it was a-- it was, in my idea-- it was-- it was not such a big hotel. It was a small hotel. And-- but for books around, I don't remember a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were very religious. My father-- my brothers had to lay tefillin every morning. And Friday evening, we had to go to synagogue, and Shabbat morning, and every holy day. And we weren't allowed to do anything. A lot of Jewish things were around the house. Were-- did we have a lot of books around the house? I don't remember at all. Now you went to high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a public high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went just a few years, and then I went to another kind of a school that was more-- everything you had to do. They learn you how to cook, and how to sew, and also the high school curriculum. You know. It was a different kind of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember when the anti-Semitism started-- when you started hearing about it or talking about it at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitism was very little in Holland before the war. And we were not-- we didn't talk too much about that. That we talked about when the German came to Holland, and that-- that what was going on. I tell you the truth. I was the youngest in the family, and they never talked much with all these things when I was there. So I remember that all the German Jews came to Holland, and they ran away from Holland and stayed in Holland, and also in our-- in my hometown. And it was-- and they stayed in the-- and we took them in to the hotel. So that's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, they came. And you know, that's what I remember very well. That's what was going on. We know. But somehow, everything was always secretive for me, because I was the girl, and it was-- you know? But anti-Semitism, I found out after the war-- but not so much that we were afraid for the Germans and they came.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one man that came to our hotel. And he say he will help my brothers get away and-- to-- to Belgium, and to France, and out of the country. And he was a liar. He just did it to get some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we give him all the time 100 guldens. And he was just-- want to get from scaring Jews what want to-- you know? So things like that-- I don't know if you call that anti-Semitism. Just he took advantage of the time where we lived in at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't know the danger-- what the-- of gas chambers and things like that. So my mother could have-- later on, perhaps, hidden by non-Jews. But we didn't-- we didn't know. You know, we-- my parents came from a very small town in Holland. And-- and they didn't have a background that-- that there should be a danger in Holland too. Because Holland was so many years without war.

speaker: interviewer
text: True.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there a family from-- from Holland went to America. And my father and mother say to each other, how can they go away from Holland and take all that money out of Holland and go away from Holland? So they were very devoted Dutch and to the queen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were very patriotic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very much so, yeah-- very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to go into hiding with your English teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I didn't go into hiding with the English teacher. What it was-- in 1941-- end of 1941, the German came to our house and say we had to go out. And it was really something meant-- a lot of people came to us. And we had to go out-- out of the hotel. And we had to go to-- went out of the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother could just take with her what she could carry. So it was very hard! Can you imagine? The only thing what-- what she was allowed to-- was allowed to take with her-- just her-- her personal things. So they put us in a very small, little house in Zwolle. And it was a very poor neighborhood. And there was no bathroom in, and there was no hot water. And there was a barrel, and they came once a week to clean it up. And the whole upstairs smelled funny then. And we had to sleep all together in one room. And it was-- it was a terrible atmosphere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was already done slowly until 1942. So there was already-- we had to wear stars. I was not-- right away-- the first thing what we did in Holland was we didn't-- we were not allowed to go to school. And we weren't allowed to go working. And we were not allowed to go on the street and talk with not-Jewish friends. And we were not allowed to do anything. And we had to be home-- 5 o'clock home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- in that time, I protested, because I didn't want to stay home all day. And I took my star off and went to another town, and helped all the people to-- when they needed-- some were sick, and I helped them. So I went by train. And on weekends, I came home to my mother. So it was-- really not that you were-- you were afraid to go on the street, that they recognize you. Because there were a lot of-- in that time-- Dutch people what went with the Germans. And a lot of traitors were there. So-- so when I was in this situation, I was standing on outside, and in the street, where we had that little house, there was a big house. One Catholic priest lived in there. And the Catholic priest came to me, and he say, what do you think how the Jews can save their life? That was already done in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say, the only way that we can be saved is when a non-Jewish family takes us in. I don't know how I came to see that man. And he came, an hour later back, and said he'd be ready tomorrow morning on 6 o'clock, with your bicycle. And we Jews had always-- everybody had-- in the-- next to the front door, a backpack with everything in there, that when they would pick us up, that-- that we had some-- everything ready. And they picked us up once before-- my brother, and my mother, and me. My father died in Holland, on a heart attack, in 1941. So-- and they sent us to the border. And the Jews what lived in Holland what-- since they were sitting in the Judenrate-- I don't know how they call it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judenrat-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they tried-- got us back to Holland. And so they got us back. So that was the first time. So I know-- you know-- you know, every day you lived in a fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the afternoon, the priest came back and said, he have a place for you. Be ready tomorrow morning. And-- and I will go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My English teacher from high school came, coincident. He was there in the morning-- the next morning. He has nothing to do with that I was hidden. That was hiding somewhere. And on that day, I say goodbye to my mother and to my brothers. And I never saw them again. That was the last time that I ever saw my family. After that, my mother went to Auschwitz. My mother-- they caught my mother on the way out also-- that she wants to hide somewhere. Somebody told me that. And the Germans got to know about it. And when she walked out of the door, they caught her. And my brothers were in a working camp. And they all went to Auschwitz. One brother was married. And-- he was-- one brother was 22. One brother was 24. My oldest brother was hidden, and he had two children at that time. And he was hidden in Utrecht.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to that brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That brother was hidden the whole war in different places. And he made it. After the war, he-- he-- in between, he had three children. He had one more child in the war. And he had-- and so-- and he made it. His children were hidden all over by non-Jewish people. And he was very bitter. I don't know what happened to the whole family. My whole family was killed, and I have not-- only this brother I had left. And the rest-- uncle-- uncle, and aunts, and everything-- everything. Everything in Holland was-- was gone. So-- and he died when he was 60. I think he went to Canada from Holland. He sold the business in Holland-- the hoteling business. And he went to Canada, and he was not successful. And he was very unhappy and very bitter. And he died when he was 60. He had a heart attack in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selma, how old were you when you went into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: 19. Can you describe what your life was like in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hidden the first time by a very nice lady. She was a nurse. And it was a little-- small, little apartment. And she was-- and there were a lot of books there I could read. And it was a very pleasant person. And that what all that I remember, that it was not so difficult for me there-- to be there. And I was there a very short time. She had a friend. She had a whole family what she wants to help. So I had to go to another family. And I went to another family outside Utrecht. I was hidden in Utrecht, and that was until-- in Bilthoven. It was a little town outside Holland-- Utrecht. And that was a very large house by a couple-- a couple and a brother. There were three-- three adults. And I had to do the cleaning. And there was no food in the house. It was already very difficult in Holland. That was already in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot that I was in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before or after you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was after. No, that was after.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was after I escaped. So-- so I was-- it was a very difficult time. It was very difficult, because the people-- there was nothing to eat. I had to do-- I was not allowed to talk with anybody. The people went home 8 o'clock out of the house, and they came, 7, 8 o'clock back home. And I was all alone. And I remember she had a bone, and I had to cook soup from it. And she say, see, you can cook soup from it. There's still a little bit fat on top. And I had to cook that all over again. And that's what I got to eat. And sometimes I took a piece of bread and hide it under my pillow for at night when I was so hungry there. And also we had apples in the attic, and I slept in the attic. And I took sometimes an apple. And I felt that I stole from her, because I was very hungry. And I was very, very lonely there. And what was now happened-- this-- one day, the man told me I can go and visit another family what had also Jews hidden. And they lived not far. It was almost next door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked to these people. And I felt very secure, because I had a passport. Every Jew in Holland had a passport with a J in it. And the underground took the J out from me. And so I felt very secure when I went there and I walked on the street. So the minute I come there, the police came. And so I say, I'm a nurse, and I come and help these people. There was another family there. And so he said, give me your passport. And he hold it to the light. And he could see the J was taken out. So I know that that was the end. So this-- what was now happened-- and then he asked me where I was hidden. I didn't want to tell them that, because, the day before, another family came-- and a couple. And I didn't want to tell him. So I thought, these people have to have time to run away. But what was now happened-- these people where I was hidden, were traitors. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after me, there were 28 Jews were there, and they all were sent to concentration camps. And nothing was happened to him. Because-- and always, when Jews were hidden by non-Jews, the-- the-- the-- the people would-- what hide them had to go-- also they went-- were sent to concentration camps. So, after the war, we found out that these people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, from there, I went to Utrecht. And sudden-- it was in Utrecht, in police station, and I couldn't get out. The police was very good. And they tried to get me out. But I didn't want to go out. I want to go to my mother. Were they Dutch police, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dutch police. Because my brother, I think, was after that, and he was already a little bit underground. And-- and they came to me. And they want to hide-- to get me out. And-- and they had a place for me. But I didn't know that the underground was that [NON-ENGLISH]-- strong. I didn't have any money. And I also wanted to go to my mother. And so my mother is in Poland in a big camp. And my brothers are there. And I will find them. And I wanted to go to my mother. That's all what I thought about it. I-- I didn't think about anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, from there, I went to jail in Amsterdam. I was three months in jail in Amsterdam. And we were together in a very small cell with-- with 10 girls-- 10-- 8 women-- all-- from all ages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a time that I really grow up in jail, when everybody tells their life story. And-- and I really was not the little girl anymore from the family. And I really had a reality what was going on in life, really. From-- and so, also, it was very coincident-- we did get very little to eat in the-- in the jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other women like that were in jail with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a professor's wife. And one was a doctor's wife. And one was a beautician. And one was just a girl from-- from-- from on the street. So everybody tells their story. And-- and-- and we-- and one woman was very beautiful. She had blonde hair. And she blonde-- she made it-- colored it in-- in jail. And she came from-- her-- her husband had a very big factory. He was very rich. And she got smuggled in all different things. She bought up the people that brought food to us and things like that-- I think. Because she got all different kinds of things in-- in to the-- to the jail. And she was very beautiful. I remember-- she was very-- a woman from-- from the world. And-- and I found her later in Vught, in another concentration camp. And she was together already with the head of the-- from the-- from the-- a Jewish man, but had also something to say. So she was a very impressive person-- and would every morning exercise, and things like that, in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- the spirit was tremendous. But nobody knows about gas chambers, you know? And-- and-- and I guess because we were Jews, our religion-- and we were born as Jew, we were in this-- in jail. So we-- we didn't think that would something serious would happen to us. And we didn't get much to eat. And I remember once, the woman what brought us the food said to me, gee, you look so familiar. Do you have a brother that was a waiter in-- in a hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my oldest brother was in hotel school and had to work in an-- in a hotel. And so, through that, we got double food. We got more food then. So that was luck in-- in jail. And from there out, we came together in-- in January, we came together in an office. And we came together with a whole bunch of Dutch girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1943. Or 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1942-- end of 1942-- beginning of 1943, perhaps. And we-- we we-- these girls-- we stick together with these girls. And that somehow saved our life. Because from there, we went to Vught. And Vught was a concentration camp in Holland. And we stayed there three months. We had it-- it was-- we didn't get much to eat, but it was not one of the worst of all. The men had-- they suffered tremendous. They had shaved the head. They were-- had heavy labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came there, somebody that came to our hotel said to me, would you like to have a job as head of the laundromat? And I took this job as head of the laundromat. So I was not so much involved in that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, in 1943, we were sent to Westerbork. Westerbork was a camp where all the Jews from Holland went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a transit camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a holding camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also the Germans what came to Holland, stayed in that camp. And it was very nice. There was already a little town. There was music, and there was dancing. And there was-- artists went there. And there were painters were there. And it was-- it still exists. And it's now a museum in Holland. So-- it was also the Germans were better. They want to stay there, the German Jews. When everybody knows, perhaps, already a little bit what was going on. Not me, because I was here-- separated from the world. And I didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came in Westerbork, there was a brother from my mother was there, with four little boys. And he tried to get me out. Your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle. But I couldn't get out, because I was punished that I was hidden. So he couldn't get me out. So we somehow met the Dutch girls. We stayed together. And then in April, beginning of April, we went on the first freight train to Poland. So we went on-- these girls-- what I told you-- what we met in jail-- somehow we stayed together. And we went also together in this freight train. We were with 60 people together in the freight train. We did get some water. And I don't remember. I remember usually we were very afraid. We didn't have anywhere to sit on. And there were a lot of old people. And there was a barrel in the corner, and that was our bathroom. And we didn't get to eat anything. I think we had something with us. But it was very scary. But it was Germans were shooting and screaming-- and next to the train, and over the train-- and not in Holland. And so quickly, when we were out of Holland, and anywhere where we stopped was just-- was just very scary. And also, it was-- where I told you, where we could only sit on the floor, so it was very hard for these older people. But we-- we were young. And we didn't know what has happened, really. We didn't. And it took three days and three nights. And when the train stopped, there was a little window in the train, and all these people standing on the railroad stations, and they did like that to us. And we thought, there is some hatred to us. They want to have us dead. But they want to tell us that we go to our death. But we didn't understand it. So, after three days and three nights, we came into Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the smells and the sights when you first entered Sobibor? Did you walk to camp from the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train stopped at a beautiful railroad station where little houses had little flowers and geraniums. And-- and we didn't think it was-- but you could see through the little window, where you had a chance to look-- when everybody wants to look. Then they throwed open this door. And there was a screaming and hitting with whips. Out! Out! Out! We all stumbled out of this big door-- you understand-- from the train. And little children got lost from the parents. And there was a little trolley that came on what is in coal mine-- this little trolley what goes over. And there they throwed in the old woman and children what got lost from the parents-- they throwed them in there. And that went straight to the gas chamber. There were-- there was a trolley, and that went straight to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just very scared. Somehow, all the Germans-- SS stayed on the side. And then I told you before, when we stayed together with these little-- with all these girls from-- the youngest was 16, and I was one of the oldest, from 20, I think. I was in between 20 years. So we pass these Germans. And before we pass the Germans, we had our backpack. Everybody has the backpack. We have to throw away. And one woman throwed away her backpack, and her baby fell. And she said, oh, my baby! And that scene-- I remember-- remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German hit him with a whip. And the blood was streaming from the face. And he say, I will take care of the baby. You go. So he put his dog in the trolley, and it went right away to the gas chamber. And we passed these Germans. And that's the first time that they took some Dutch people out of the trail-- train. And he'd say to all of us, go on the side. And that was our luck. We were all young. And they all told us, go on the side. And we had no idea what they want with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we saw all the women and children walking-- right. And they went to the gas chamber. And then the men were sitting on the side. And they had to take their shoes off and had to band them with the shoelaces together. Because, later on, I know that everything was sent to Germany. So when the shoelace is not together, then they get the shoe separated. So.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I heard a German speaking to them. Everybody got a little card. And the German spoke to them. You're here in Sobibor. And we will take care of you, and-- and write to home that you came safe here. And the Dutch people did. The Dutch people didn't know. They-- when they passed the Germans, they took their hat off when they had a hat on. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were still pretending you were going to like a work camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah! Of course! Nobody-- the Dutch transport didn't know anything. They didn't know anything what-- what was going on-- definitely none-- none from the Dutch people. They just walked to the gas chamber. But I remember one day, I see so for me that it was-- they said to the-- that you're going to take a shower. And they had took all the clothes already off-- when they had to put it together on the-- there with the banded shoes. And they run. And they say, come, come, come, we take a shower! To-- they're screaming to each other. And all the men were running to the shower. I just that I see so-- how it was happened today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because where I worked was a window. And you could see when a transport came. So when a transport came, then was this fire. And then it was-- the smell and things like that.
